subject: geography
title: KS3 Geography - Rocks and Geology 1 Reading
description: Reading passages for KS3 geography revision on what rocks are and how the main rock groups form. The passages reinforce rock properties, examples, and key formation processes from the source pages.

data.sourcePassage: All rocks are made from minerals. The minerals inside a rock can have different shapes, sizes, and colours, and the way they fit together affects the rock's properties. Some rocks are porous, so they have spaces between grains and can hold water. Others are non-porous because their grains fit tightly together. Geographers also describe some rocks as permeable or impermeable, depending on whether water can pass through them. Looking closely at a rock helps you work out how it formed and what it may be used for.
data.targetPassage: All rocks are made from minerals. The minerals inside a rock can have different shapes, sizes, and colours, and the way they fit together affects the rock's properties. Some rocks are porous, so they have spaces between grains and can hold water. Others are non-porous because their grains fit tightly together. Geographers also describe some rocks as permeable or impermeable, depending on whether water can pass through them. Looking closely at a rock helps you work out how it formed and what it may be used for.

questions.question: What does porous mean in this passage?
options: The rock has spaces between grains that can hold water.
The rock is made from lava.
The rock has changed by heat and pressure.
questions.modelAnswer: A porous rock has spaces between grains that can hold water.

questions.question: Why can two rocks made from minerals still behave differently?
questions.modelAnswer: They can behave differently because the minerals have different sizes, shapes, and colours and fit together in different ways.

questions.question: Why is it useful to know if a rock is permeable or impermeable?
questions.modelAnswer: It helps explain whether water can move through the rock, which affects drainage and how the rock may be used.

data.sourcePassage: Sedimentary rocks form from deposits that were originally eroded from older rocks or from the remains of living things. First, weathering and erosion break material into smaller pieces. Next, rivers, wind, waves, the sea, or ice transport the sediment. The sediment is then deposited in places such as river beds, lakes, shallow seas, or hollows in the landscape. As more layers build up, the weight increases and compacts the lower layers. In the spaces between sediment grains, dissolved minerals harden and cement the material together. Once this happens, the sediment is lithified and has become sedimentary rock.
data.targetPassage: Sedimentary rocks form from deposits that were originally eroded from older rocks or from the remains of living things. First, weathering and erosion break material into smaller pieces. Next, rivers, wind, waves, the sea, or ice transport the sediment. The sediment is then deposited in places such as river beds, lakes, shallow seas, or hollows in the landscape. As more layers build up, the weight increases and compacts the lower layers. In the spaces between sediment grains, dissolved minerals harden and cement the material together. Once this happens, the sediment is lithified and has become sedimentary rock.

questions.question: Name one force that can transport sediment.
questions.modelAnswer: One force that can transport sediment is rivers.

questions.question: What happens after sediment is deposited in layers?
options: More layers increase the weight and compact the lower sediment.
The sediment immediately melts into magma.
The layers turn straight into marble.
The grains become larger because of eruption.
questions.modelAnswer: More layers build up and compact the lower sediment.

questions.question: Why are dissolved minerals important in sedimentary rock formation?
questions.modelAnswer: They harden in the spaces between grains and cement the sediment together.

questions.question: Why does this process show that sedimentary rock often forms over time rather than instantly?
questions.modelAnswer: It takes time for sediment to be eroded, transported, deposited in layers, compacted, and cemented together.

data.sourcePassage: Igneous rocks form when magma cools and solidifies. Deep inside the Earth, temperatures are high enough to melt rock into magma. Because it is very hot, magma tends to rise upward through the crust. If it cools below the surface, it is called intrusive rock and usually cools slowly. Slow cooling gives crystals time to grow, which is why rocks such as granite have large visible crystals. If the magma reaches the surface, it is called lava. Lava cools much more quickly, so extrusive rocks such as basalt often have much smaller crystals.
data.targetPassage: Igneous rocks form when magma cools and solidifies. Deep inside the Earth, temperatures are high enough to melt rock into magma. Because it is very hot, magma tends to rise upward through the crust. If it cools below the surface, it is called intrusive rock and usually cools slowly. Slow cooling gives crystals time to grow, which is why rocks such as granite have large visible crystals. If the magma reaches the surface, it is called lava. Lava cools much more quickly, so extrusive rocks such as basalt often have much smaller crystals.

questions.question: What is magma called after it reaches the Earth's surface?
questions.modelAnswer: Magma at the Earth's surface is called lava.

questions.question: Why does granite usually have larger crystals than basalt?
options: Granite forms from sediment on the sea floor.
questions.modelAnswer: Granite usually has larger crystals because it cools more slowly underground.

questions.question: What is the difference between intrusive and extrusive igneous rocks?
questions.modelAnswer: Intrusive rocks cool below the Earth's surface, while extrusive rocks cool at the surface after eruption.

questions.question: How does cooling rate affect the size of crystals in igneous rocks?
questions.modelAnswer: Slow cooling lets larger crystals grow, while quick cooling leads to smaller crystals.

data.sourcePassage: Metamorphic rocks form when heat and pressure change existing rocks. Movements inside the Earth can bury rocks deeply, squeeze them, or raise their temperature without melting them. In these conditions, minerals in the original rock react and can rearrange into new minerals. New crystals may grow, but the rock does not become liquid again. That is why metamorphic rocks do not form from new melted material; they form from older sedimentary or igneous rocks. Common examples include marble, slate, schist, and gneiss. Studying these rocks helps geographers understand how powerful forces inside the Earth can reshape the crust.
data.targetPassage: Metamorphic rocks form when heat and pressure change existing rocks. Movements inside the Earth can bury rocks deeply, squeeze them, or raise their temperature without melting them. In these conditions, minerals in the original rock react and can rearrange into new minerals. New crystals may grow, but the rock does not become liquid again. That is why metamorphic rocks do not form from new melted material; they form from older sedimentary or igneous rocks. Common examples include marble, slate, schist, and gneiss. Studying these rocks helps geographers understand how powerful forces inside the Earth can reshape the crust.

questions.question: What two main conditions change rock into metamorphic rock?
questions.modelAnswer: The two main conditions are heat and pressure.

questions.question: Which statement is correct about metamorphic rocks?
options: They form from older rocks that are changed without melting.
They always form from lava at the surface.
They only form in rivers and shallow seas.
questions.modelAnswer: Metamorphic rocks form from older rocks that are changed without melting.

questions.question: Why is it important that the rock does not melt during metamorphism?
questions.modelAnswer: If it melted, it would form magma and could become igneous rock instead of metamorphic rock.

questions.question: How do metamorphic rocks show the effect of forces inside the Earth?
questions.modelAnswer: They show that burial, squeezing, and heating inside the Earth can change the minerals and structure of existing rock.
